prevalence.source: Liga Brasileira de Epilepsia / epidemiological studies and IHME GBD estimates
prevalence.trend: stable; treatment gap persists in vulnerable populations

incidence.source: IHME Global Burden of Disease (GBD) / regional studies

burden.mortalityRate: Elevated vs general population (SUDEP and injury-related)

riskFactors: substance use and sleep deprivation triggers
vulnerablePopulations: rural populations with limited neurology access
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical diagnosis per ILAE (≥2 unprovoked seizures or one seizure with high recurrence risk); EEG and neuroimaging (MRI/CT) as available

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no screening; case-based diagnosis in primary care with referral when available
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: ~60-70% seizure control with appropriate therapy; treatment gap remains in underserved areas

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: EEG/imaging access variable across SUS; specialist neurology more concentrated in capitals
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: First-line antiseizure medications (e.g., carbamazepine, valproate, levetiracetam in many settings); epilepsy surgery evaluation in reference centers for drug-resistant epilepsy
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Treatment gap and delayed specialist evaluation, stigma, and uneven access to newer antiseizure medications and surgery programs
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: SUS provides core antiseizure medications and has specialized epilepsy centers in major cities
